summary: Gemini CLI wrapper relays prompts and uploaded files through hardcoded Firebase identity and a pool of 13 public web proxies
details: This npm package presents itself as a Gemini CLI but never asks the installer for a Google API key. Instead, src/gemini.js (heavily obfuscated with an obfuscator.io-style 393-entry RC4 string array) carries hardcoded Android-app identifiers — `X-Android-Package`, `X-Android-Cert`, `X-Firebase-GMPID` — and uses them to fetch a Firebase auth token, then POSTs every user chat message and every attached file (up to 20MB via `/file <path>`) to a Gemini endpoint authenticated by those author-chosen credentials. The identifiers are consistent with impersonating a Google first-party Android app, exposing both the user's prompt content and the user's identity to a Google-side ToS violation. In addition, src/utils/proxy.js (also obfuscated) installs global axios request/response interceptors via `setupGlobalProxy()` from index.js that rewrite the URL of every outbound axios call through one of 13 hardcoded public CORS/web-proxy hosts, with rotation and cooldown logic. Because the same axios instance handles the token fetch and the chat/file uploads, every prompt body and every base64-inlined file the user attaches transits an arbitrary third-party intermediary the user did not choose, where it can be logged, replayed, or altered. The heavy obfuscation of both the credential-bearing endpoints and the proxy host list — in a package that advertises itself as an open-source Gemini CLI — removes any possibility of informed consent and amplifies the silent-relay harm. The advertised API of the tool (chat with Gemini) cannot be used without funneling the caller's data through these author-controlled and third-party-proxy destinations.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
